topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. My stomach clenched instantly, a familiar, tight knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a request. From Mark. The name sent a cold wave washing over me, chilling the warmth that had been trying to settle in my chest. I stared at the profile picture—a slightly too-wide grin that used to feel like a physical assault. A slow, deliberate breath escaped my lips, tasting like dust. I didn't click it. Instead, I minimized the message, focusing intently on the pattern of the cracks in the ceiling plaster. Every muscle in my jaw felt locked, resisting the urge to flinch away from whatever ghost had just materialized on my digital doorstep.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, the glass cool against my palm, and the sight of the incoming request made my hand tremble slightly. It was him. Liam. The memory of him—the sharp words, the way he’d always managed to find the vulnerable edges of things—flashed behind my eyes, bringing a dull ache to my temples. I watched the little red dot pulse on the screen, a tiny beacon of unwelcome attention. My shoulders slumped, sinking further into the worn armchair. I didn't bother to read the name or the accompanying message. I just slid the phone face down on the table, the sound of the screen clicking off sounding disproportionately loud in the silence of my room. A heavy sigh escaped me, a sound more like deflation than release.

[story 3]

When the alert chimed, I froze mid-scroll. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I registered the source of the contact. It was Alex. The thought of him brought a sudden, prickly sensation across my skin, like static electricity building before a storm. I leaned back against the cool wall, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, a defensive posture instantly adopted. My gaze drifted towards the window, watching the streetlights blur through the damp air. A strange, hollow feeling settled deep in my core, a pervasive sense of weariness that seemed to seep into my bones. I didn't move to check the notification; I just let the tension coil tighter, a silent acknowledgment of the unwelcome intrusion.
